name: auth
context: Authenticate GitLab API and CLI access with personal, project, group, OAuth, and CI job tokens
info: GitLab authentication affects every automated action in this ecosystem. Agents need to know which token type to use, which header to send, how self-managed hostnames alter requests, and how the official `glab` CLI resolves environment variables and stored credentials. This node covers personal, project, and group access tokens, OAuth bearer tokens, CI job tokens, `glab auth login`, and the environment variables that make automation portable across GitLab.com and self-managed instances.
triggers: use glab with a self-managed host
pick the right gitlab token type
body:
# GitLab Authentication

GitLab supports several token types, and the correct one depends on where automation runs and how much scope it needs. The REST authentication guide recommends personal, project, or group access tokens in the `PRIVATE-TOKEN` header for most API calls, OAuth tokens in `Authorization: Bearer`, and `JOB-TOKEN` for CI jobs that should stay short-lived.

```bash
curl --header 'PRIVATE-TOKEN: $GITLAB_TOKEN' \
  'https://gitlab.example.com/api/v4/projects'

curl --header 'Authorization: Bearer $OAUTH_TOKEN' \
  'https://gitlab.example.com/api/v4/projects'
```

For the official CLI, `glab auth login` stores credentials and respects `GITLAB_TOKEN`, `GITLAB_ACCESS_TOKEN`, or `OAUTH_TOKEN` if they are already set. `GITLAB_HOST` or `GL_HOST` selects the instance for self-managed automation. Load [[gitlab/projects]] or another executable node after authentication is settled.

sections.title: Token types and headers
sections.body: Use personal, project, or group access tokens for most REST automation and send them as `PRIVATE-TOKEN: <token>`. OAuth 2.0 access tokens use the standard `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header. Project and group access tokens are useful when the automation should belong to the resource instead of a human account. For least surprise, standardize on `GITLAB_TOKEN` for scripts that send the `PRIVATE-TOKEN` header.

sections.title: CI job tokens
sections.body: GitLab generates `CI_JOB_TOKEN` for each running job. The token is short-lived, revoked when the job finishes, and intentionally narrower than a personal access token. Use the `JOB-TOKEN` header when calling endpoints that job tokens can access. Cross-project access is usually restricted by the target project's allowlist, so job-token-based automation fails until the target project explicitly trusts the source project or group.

sections.title: glab login and environment variables
sections.body: Use `glab auth login --hostname gitlab.example.org --token <token>` for non-interactive setup, or `glab auth login --hostname $CI_SERVER_HOST --job-token $CI_JOB_TOKEN` inside CI. `glab` respects `GITLAB_TOKEN`, `GITLAB_ACCESS_TOKEN`, and `OAUTH_TOKEN` before stored credentials. `GITLAB_HOST` or `GL_HOST` points the CLI at self-managed instances; if omitted, `glab` defaults to `https://gitlab.com`.

sections.title: OAuth and delegated access
sections.body: Use OAuth when an agent acts on behalf of a user and should participate in browser-based authorization or token refresh. The API accepts OAuth bearer tokens in the `Authorization` header. This is the right fit for user-facing integrations, while access tokens are simpler for service automation and repository maintenance scripts.
